annotation: The model incorrectly treated the filer as a qualifying surviving spouse and used joint Social Security thresholds, deductions, and rates. Single-filer treatment produces $18,542.70 of taxable Social Security and uses $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model included nontaxable veterans benefits in gross income, treated employer-sponsored premiums as an AGI deduction despite no wages, and then asserted that deductions eliminated tax despite its own positive taxable-income calculations. The correct taxable income is $26,095.78, leaving $2,883.49 of tax.

The model correctly selected single filing but used the maximum 85% of Social Security rather than the statutory worksheet result of $18,542.70. It also chose an aged standard deduction instead of the larger $18,341.33 itemized deduction, overstating taxable income and tax.

The model incorrectly assigned qualifying-surviving-spouse treatment and then claimed roughly $40,000 of deductions made taxable income near zero. The computation instead uses single-filer Social Security rules and $24,341.33 of total deductions, leaving $26,095.78 taxable.

The model used qualifying-surviving-spouse filing status, joint Social Security thresholds, and the joint standard deduction. The tax unit is treated as single, with $18,542.70 of taxable Social Security and itemized deductions of $18,341.33 plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model's stated inputs imply about $2,023 of tax, but it submitted $3,204 by inventing an unexplained adjustment for higher brackets even though its taxable income remained within the 10% joint bracket. It also used qualifying-surviving-spouse rules instead of the single-filer derivation.

The model treated 85% of Social Security as taxable and used only the aged single standard deduction. The Social Security worksheet yields $18,542.70, and the filer uses $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model incorrectly assumed a TCJA sunset, deducted a personal exemption, and treated employer-sponsored premiums as deductible medical spending. The applicable computation instead itemizes $18,341.33 and separately deducts $6,000 for the senior deduction, with no personal exemption.

The submitted $1,109 is incompatible with the model's stated $37,990 of taxable income under any applicable 2026 single-filer bracket calculation. The correct derivation has $26,095.78 of taxable income and tax of $2,883.49.

The model applied an inapplicable personal exemption and pre-TCJA 10% and 15% brackets. The correct deduction is $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction, followed by the applicable 2026 brackets.

The model assumed reverted pre-TCJA law, deducted a personal exemption, and counted only $16,470 of itemized deductions. The engine applies $18,341.33 of itemized deductions and the separate $6,000 senior deduction, with current 2026 rates.

The model correctly calculated the Social Security inclusion and single status but chose a projected standard deduction because it imposed a $10,000 SALT cap. The applicable itemized deduction is $18,341.33, and adding the $6,000 senior deduction reduces taxable income to $26,095.78.

The model asserted that age-related and surviving-spouse deductions fully offset income without computing them. The deductions total $24,341.33 against AGI of $50,437.11, so $26,095.78 remains taxable.

The model incorrectly claimed the aged standard deduction eliminated pension, IRA, interest, and taxable Social Security income. The applicable itemized and senior deductions leave $26,095.78 of taxable income rather than zero.

The model used qualifying-surviving-spouse Social Security thresholds and the joint standard deduction, producing only $11,542 of taxable Social Security. Single treatment produces $18,542.70, and the filer takes $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model was close on taxable Social Security but used an age-based standard deduction instead of the $18,341.33 itemized deduction. That deduction choice fixes taxable income at $26,095.78 and tax at $2,883.49.

The model correctly identified taxable Social Security and AGI but used $24,150 as the combined aged-standard and senior deductions. The correct combined deduction is $24,341.33 because itemized deductions are $18,341.33 before the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model's $359 answer reflects surviving-spouse deductions and rate treatment that reduce taxable income to the 10% bracket floor. Single-filer treatment and the actual $24,341.33 deduction total leave $26,095.78 taxable.

The model asserted that senior deductions and credits exceeded taxable income without calculating either. No credit eliminates the liability, and the allowed deductions leave $26,095.78 subject to tax.

The model incorrectly invoked post-TCJA personal exemptions and 10%/15% brackets while combining them with qualifying-surviving-spouse language. The correct computation uses $18,341.33 of itemized deductions, the $6,000 senior deduction, and the applicable 2026 brackets.

The model treated employer-sponsored insurance premiums as deductible medical expenses and applied assumed post-TCJA 10%/15% rates. The correct itemized deduction is $18,341.33, followed by the separate $6,000 senior deduction and applicable 2026 brackets.
The model supplied neither a numeric output nor an explanation for the requested variable, so its response failed the required output contract.

The model asserted that the elderly credit and age-related deductions reduced the liability to zero, despite also stating substantial taxable income. No usable nonrefundable credit applies, and deductions leave $26,095.78 taxable.

The model omitted taxable Social Security by ignoring tax-exempt interest and half of Social Security in provisional income and by using qualifying-widow treatment. Provisional income is $50,520, yielding $18,542.70 of taxable Social Security under the single-filer worksheet.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The model correctly stated that no earned wages were provided and that Social Security benefits are not employee-payroll-taxable, but then submitted $1,534 instead of the zero implied by its own rule. Its numeric answer is consistent with applying payroll tax to a nonwage retirement-income component rather than limiting the tax base to listed wages and covered earned compensation.

The model did not provide a parseable payroll_tax value or explanation. The correct derivation uses the listed income categories and finds no wage, salary, or covered earned income, leaving the employee payroll tax base at zero.

The model applied a projected post-TCJA joint standard deduction plus personal exemptions totaling only $28,350, leaving $3,052.25 of taxable income. PolicyEngine's 2026 joint standard deduction exceeds the household's $31,402.21 AGI, so the model created taxable income by using the wrong deduction regime.

The model used a $29,600 deduction amount made from a post-TCJA-style standard deduction and personal exemptions, which left $1,802.25 of taxable income. The correct computation applies the 2026 PolicyEngine standard deduction for joint filers, which is larger than the $31,402.21 AGI and reduces taxable income to zero.

The model overstated taxable Social Security by treating $11,588 of benefits as taxable instead of the $2,338 included in PolicyEngine's AGI, inflating AGI to $40,652. It also used a much too low post-TCJA-style senior standard deduction of about $20,400, while PolicyEngine's 2026 joint standard deduction eliminates taxable income entirely.

The model incorrectly converted age 65+ into ABD Medicaid eligibility and then relied on medically needy or Medicare Savings Program concepts that are not the eligibility category PolicyEngine assigned. It also misapplied SSI-related counting by treating Social Security as largely excluded and concluding the couple passed the aged pathway, while the engine trace shows the SSI pathway fails both income and asset tests and the category is NONE.

The model treated being 71 as making the head potentially Medicaid eligible without applying any Virginia pathway test. The correct derivation checks actual categories: MAGI categories fail at 2.05 FPL, the SSI-related aged pathway fails, and no other category is identified.

The model incorrectly treated Medicare eligibility plus moderate income as qualifying the head through a Medicare Savings Program Medicaid pathway. PolicyEngine's category computation assigns NONE because the head is above the relevant MAGI limits and does not satisfy the SSI-related income and asset tests.

The model correctly computed the two employee FICA components as $1,801.968 and $421.428 but submitted $4,412.328 instead of their sum. It added an extra amount inconsistent with its own stated no-spouse-wages and no-Additional-Medicare-Tax reasoning, effectively doubling the employee-side payroll tax calculation.

The model identified the right tax base and included Social Security plus Medicare with no Additional Medicare Tax, but it did not apply the full 7.65% employee FICA rate to $29,064. The correct computation is $1,801.97 of Social Security tax plus $421.43 of Medicare tax, while $2,090 is an undercalculated shortcut rather than the required component sum.

The model used the right 7.65% employee FICA rate and the right $29,064 wage base, but it rounded the result to $2,220 instead of carrying the payroll tax calculation to dollars and cents. PolicyEngine computes the Social Security and Medicare components separately and rounds to $1,801.97 + $421.43 = $2,223.39.

The model bypassed PolicyEngine's gross-income eligibility screen and treated the elderly household as qualifying under a net-income calculation after deductions. It also converted a mortgage balance into a shelter deduction even though no mortgage payment, rent, property tax, or utility expense was listed, producing an invented positive monthly benefit instead of applying the gross-income cutoff that yields $0.

The model invented an aged pathway based on the spouse's lack of own earnings and ignored that Medicaid eligibility is not determined on separate spousal income alone. It also contradicted its own MAGI calculation: the household's MAGI exceeds the expansion threshold, and the spouse has no SSI-related categorical route in the engine.

The model misapplied Virginia ABD Medicaid by treating age 65+ plus modest assets as enough for eligibility. PolicyEngine did not place the spouse in an ABD, SSI, blind, disabled, dependent, or MAGI Medicaid category, and the household MAGI is 2.05 x FPL, above the MAGI-based limits.

The model used a generic shortcut that a 69-year-old is potentially Medicaid eligible without applying any Virginia pathway. The correct computation requires an actual eligibility category, and the spouse qualifies through none of the MAGI or non-MAGI pathways.

The model confused Medicare age with Medicaid eligibility and asserted Medicare Savings Program eligibility without checking the applicable category and income test. PolicyEngine assigns the spouse no Medicaid category, and the household's income level of 2.05 x FPL blocks MAGI-based Medicaid eligibility.

The model added an unlisted 40-quarter work-history confirmation requirement to the PolicyEngine Medicare eligibility variable. Under the reference rule, the spouse's age 69 alone satisfies Medicare eligibility, so treating missing work credits or Social Security receipt as disqualifying produced the wrong 0 answer.

The model allowed only $21,484 of total Virginia deductions and exemptions, omitting part of the deduction stack available to two spouses over age 65. After excluding Social Security and applying both age deductions, the joint standard deduction, and both personal exemptions, no Virginia taxable income remains.

The model’s unexplained $560 liability implies that it left positive Virginia taxable income after applying only a partial set of deductions. The two age deductions, joint standard deduction, personal exemptions, and Virginia Social Security subtraction eliminate taxable income.

The model incorrectly reduced the head’s Virginia age deduction by Social Security benefits and therefore allowed only the spouse’s $12,000 age deduction. Social Security is subtracted from Virginia income; it does not offset the head’s age deduction, so both qualifying age deductions apply and eliminate taxable income with the other deductions and exemptions.

The model said it applied the Social Security exclusion, both age deductions, and both personal exemptions but produced a positive tax inconsistent with that deduction stack. Those adjustments together with the joint standard deduction reduce Virginia taxable income to zero, leaving no amount to apply the tax brackets to.

The model treated the full $15,225 of Social Security as Virginia income, used conflicting estimated standard deductions, and submitted a number that does not match any of its own bracket calculations. Virginia excludes Social Security, and the resulting wage-based income is fully offset by both age deductions, the joint standard deduction, and personal exemptions.

The model applied single-filer Social Security thresholds, a single-filer standard deduction, and single-filer brackets even though the household is a joint return. Under the married-joint Social Security calculation, only $4,390 of Social Security is taxable, and the joint deduction with two senior additions eliminates all taxable income.

The model improperly included the $24,000 disability benefit in AGI, overstated taxable Social Security at $23,663, and used an obsolete deduction scheme combining a $15,650 standard deduction with personal exemptions. The correct AGI is $23,130, which is fully absorbed by the 2026 joint standard deduction and two senior additions.

The model treated the $24,000 disability benefit as taxable and overstated taxable Social Security at $23,663. Excluding the disability benefit and applying the joint Social Security formula yields $23,130 of AGI, below the applicable joint deduction for two spouses over 65.

The model treated all $44,080 of Social Security as taxable instead of applying the married-joint provisional-income formula. Only $4,390 of Social Security enters AGI, so AGI is $23,130 and the senior-adjusted joint standard deduction leaves no taxable income.

The model asserted $22,203 of taxable income after deductions, which requires overstating taxable income sources or understating the joint deduction. The correct income inclusion is $18,340 of pension, $400 of interest, and $4,390 of taxable Social Security, while the $24,000 disability benefit is excluded; the resulting $23,130 AGI is entirely deducted.

The model expressly included disability income and taxable Social Security in a positive joint taxable-income calculation. The disability benefit is excluded, only $4,390 of Social Security is taxable, and the joint standard deduction plus both age additions reduces the resulting $23,130 AGI to zero taxable income.

The model improperly included the $24,000 disability benefit in AGI and calculated $37,468 of taxable Social Security from a provisional-income base containing that excluded benefit. The correct joint calculation includes only $4,390 of Social Security, producing $23,130 of AGI that is fully offset by the deduction for a joint return with both spouses over 65.

Although the model ultimately excluded the disability benefit, it treated all $44,080 of Social Security as taxable merely because combined income crossed the joint threshold. Crossing the threshold does not make every dollar taxable: the joint formula yields $4,390 of taxable Social Security, and the resulting $23,130 AGI is below the senior-adjusted joint standard deduction.

The model correctly stated that no wages were provided and that Social Security retirement is not subject to payroll tax, then contradicted that rule by imposing $1,926 on unspecified wage-equivalent income. The prompt requires unlisted numeric inputs to be treated as zero, so there is no taxable payroll base for Social Security, Medicare, Additional Medicare Tax, or Washington employee payroll taxes.

The model treated age 75 plus no own income as sufficient for an aged Medicaid pathway, but PolicyEngine assigned the spouse medicaid_category = NONE. It also ignored the household MAGI income level of 2.90 FPL for MAGI categories and substituted an individual-income shortcut that the reference trace does not use for eligibility.

Its reasoning actually derives an estimate near $22,379, but it submits $27,049 without any computation supporting that value. It also omits the full $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions, leaving AGI and taxable income too high.

It taxed only $23,384 of Social Security instead of $50,583.25 and improperly deducted a $23,926 traditional 401(k) contribution despite zero listed wages. It also invented nonrefundable credits and treated a 64-year-old as over 65.

Its displayed calculation produces roughly $22,652, but it submits $19,524 with no supporting adjustment. It also omits the trace's $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions and substitutes estimated bracket thresholds.
It omitted the $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions, using AGI of $183,291 instead of $182,172.27. It then applied estimated rather than actual 2026 MFJ bracket thresholds, overstating tax.

It used an estimated $30,000 standard deduction instead of $32,200 and estimated the 2026 bracket thresholds. Those choices raised taxable income and ordinary income tax above the trace-derived amounts.

It applied only a $1,118 IRA adjustment and mis-added pre-Social-Security income, rather than using the full $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions that produce AGI of $182,172.27. It then rounded an approximate bracket calculation to $22,000 instead of computing the exact tax.

It incorrectly assumed pre-TCJA law for 2026, using personal exemptions, a $15,000 standard deduction, and 10%/15%/25% brackets. The applicable computation uses a $32,200 MFJ standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and the 2026 10%/12%/22% brackets at this income.

It incorrectly used a $16,000 standard deduction, $10,000 of personal exemptions, and pre-TCJA 10%/15%/25% brackets. The correct 2026 calculation uses the $32,200 MFJ standard deduction and current-law brackets.

It treated approximately $183,865 as taxable income even after claiming to account for the standard deduction. The correct derivation reduces $182,172.27 of AGI by $32,200, yielding taxable income of $149,972.27.

It assumed TCJA expiration and applied personal exemptions plus post-sunset rate parameters. The 2026 computation instead uses a $32,200 MFJ standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and current-law brackets.

It incorrectly applied post-TCJA-sunset rules, including a $16,100 standard deduction, $10,100 of personal exemptions, and pre-TCJA brackets. The applicable 2026 parameters produce a $32,200 standard deduction and $149,972.27 of taxable income.

It mishandled the net capital loss by using a $3,000 loss instead of the actual $1,582 net loss and invented a $500 sick-spouse credit. Its stated tax after credits is $28,867, yet it submits $49,523.45 without any supporting computation.

It incorrectly asserted that age-based and itemized deductions eliminate the tax. Neither spouse is 65, medical expenses do not clear the 7.5%-of-AGI floor, and the $32,200 standard deduction leaves $149,972.27 taxable income.

Its $2,320 result implies that it deducted retirement contributions or medical expenses far beyond the allowable amounts. The trace leaves $149,972.27 taxable after the standard deduction, producing $21,590.70 of ordinary tax plus $564 of preferential-rate tax.
It used taxable income of about $151,091 instead of $149,972.27, omitting $1,118 of the above-the-line deductions reflected in the trace. That excess taxable income drives its $246.30 overstatement.

Its result reflects taxable income near $151,090.50 rather than the trace-derived $149,972.27. It omitted $1,118 of above-the-line deductions, overstating income taxed in the 22% bracket by that amount.
It used taxable income of about $151,091 instead of $149,972.27. The $1,118 excess shows that it failed to apply that portion of the trace's above-the-line deductions before calculating tax.
It calculated from $151,090.50 of taxable income rather than $149,972.27. The difference is the omitted $1,118 above-the-line deduction, which it consequently taxed at 22%.

It treated desired retirement contributions and unspecified deductions as offsets sufficient to erase tax. With no listed wages those desired workplace deferrals do not shelter the retirement distributions, and the allowable deductions still leave $149,972.27 taxable.

It incorrectly assumed post-TCJA-sunset law, applying a $16,100 standard deduction, personal exemptions, and 10%/15%/25% brackets. The applicable 2026 calculation uses a $32,200 MFJ standard deduction and current-law brackets.

It used inflation-adjusted pre-TCJA parameters, including personal exemptions, a $17,103 standard deduction, and 10%/15%/25% brackets. Those are not the applicable 2026 parameters; the correct standard deduction is $32,200 and the ordinary brackets yield $21,590.70.

It incorrectly projected a TCJA sunset and used a $16,950 standard deduction, two personal exemptions, and pre-TCJA brackets. The 2026 computation uses a $32,200 MFJ standard deduction without personal exemptions and current-law rates.

It defaulted to zero because wages were absent, ignoring the explicitly listed taxable IRA distributions, pension, interest, dividends, and taxable Social Security. Those income items yield $149,972.27 of taxable income and a positive $22,154.70 liability.

It failed to subtract the $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions, incorrectly treated the head as age 65+, and misclassified gross income as AGI. Its narrative calculates $25,531.98 but submits $41,228.50, a value unsupported by its own arithmetic.

The model replaced the actual Medicare age test with a near-term eligibility concept. Age 64 does not satisfy the age-65 Medicare threshold for the modeled year, and no non-age Medicare pathway is listed.

The model stated the correct rule that age 64 is below the Medicare eligibility age of 65, then submitted the opposite binary value. Its error is a parse/consistency failure within the substantive answer: the explanation supports not eligible, while the submitted value marks eligible.

The model projected Medicare eligibility from the future age-65 threshold instead of applying eligibility to the constant full-year age facts. The head remains age 64 in the 2026 facts, so the standard age pathway is not met and no alternative Medicare pathway is present.

The model invented W-2 wages from weekly hours and an assumed $10 hourly wage despite the prompt saying unlisted numeric inputs are 0. It then applied Social Security and Medicare tax to those fabricated wages, and its arithmetic also double-counted an unexplained $947.17 component after already computing FICA on the same estimated wage base.

The model annualized labor earnings from hours worked even though no hourly wage or wage amount was provided and unlisted numeric inputs are 0. Payroll tax applies only to actual taxable employee earnings, so applying 6.2% Social Security and 1.45% Medicare rates to inferred wages produced a nonzero liability where the wage base is $0.

The model correctly identified that Texas has no general state individual income tax, then overrode that rule by adding an unsupported $280 residual for a nonstandard state component. The requested variable excludes local income and payroll taxes, and Texas contributes $0 to state income tax before refundable credits for this household.

The model put the full $51,475 Social Security benefit into AGI as if it were ordinary taxable income, then applied the standard deduction to that inflated AGI. It also contradicted itself on the tax calculation, moving from about $2,787 to $3,280 to a submitted $5,885 without a valid credit or surtax step.

The model correctly identified that taxable income is $0 after excluding Social Security under the provisional-income test and applying the standard deduction, then abandoned that computation. Its submitted $4,600 comes from inventing unlisted wage income from the 40-hour work facts, even though the prompt says unlisted numeric inputs are 0 and no wages are listed.

The submitted $3,144 reflects taxing a positive amount of Social Security-related income after a standard deduction rather than applying the provisional-income exclusion. With provisional income below the married-filing-jointly threshold, none of the Social Security benefit is taxable and the remaining $1,065 of non-Social-Security income is below the standard deduction.

The model stated the correct Social Security rule, then computed taxable income as $21,590 by effectively subtracting the standard deduction from gross income that still included Social Security. Once Social Security is excluded from AGI, only $1,065 remains before the standard deduction, so taxable income and pre-refundable-credit federal income tax are both $0.

The model computed MAGI as $1,065 by effectively ignoring the head's $51,475 of Social Security retirement income and taxable interest, then compared that understated income to the 138% FPL adult expansion limit. The correct engine trace has MAGI at 2.39 times FPL, so New York's expansion Medicaid pathway is income-ineligible and no other Medicaid category applies.

The model overrode the age-65 Medicare rule with an unsupported appeal to unspecified “programs” and “internal logic.” The prompt lists age 62 and no Medicare-qualifying disability, ESRD, ALS, or other qualifying status, so the head fails the Medicare eligibility test.

The model's reasoning states the correct Medicare result: age 62 is below the standard eligibility age and the expected modeled value is 0. It then submitted value = 1, making the final parsed answer contradict its own explanation rather than reflecting a substantive eligibility calculation.

The model treated Social Security retirement receipt at age 62 as conferring Medicare eligibility. Early Social Security retirement benefits do not make a person Medicare eligible before age 65 absent a qualifying disability, ESRD, ALS, or similar pathway, none of which is listed for this head.

The model's explanation correctly states that unlisted wages are zero and that Social Security retirement income produces no employee payroll tax, which supports payroll_tax = 0. It then submitted 5108 anyway, so the failure is an internal output/explanation mismatch rather than a substantive tax-rule calculation.

The model invented a very low spouse MAGI of $1,065 and treated that as below 138% FPL for a two-person household. The engine's MAGI calculation is 2.39 times FPL, so the spouse fails the ACA expansion adult income test and has no other Medicaid category.

The model treated most of the head's Social Security benefits as taxable income for New York, even though New York excludes Social Security retirement benefits from state taxable income. It also applied a contradictory bracket calculation to only $4,121 of claimed taxable income, producing a tax far above what New York rates can generate on that base.

The model correctly noted that New York excludes Social Security and that listed non-excluded income is near zero after deductions, then abandoned the prompt's rule that unlisted numeric inputs are zero by inventing wages from full-time hours. Its $3,200 answer comes from applying New York rates to an assumed wage-based income base rather than the household's listed income.

The model gave no usable computation and returned a positive New York income tax despite the listed New York taxable income being eliminated by the married-filing-joint standard deduction and exemptions. Its answer is consistent with applying NY rates to a fabricated positive taxable-income amount rather than excluding Social Security and treating unlisted wages as zero.

The model included the full Social Security retirement benefit in federal AGI for the New York taxable-income build-up and then produced a positive New York taxable income despite acknowledging Social Security is not taxed by New York. It also treated traditional IRA contributions as an add-back and applied brackets to an overstated taxable-income base, yielding a nonzero state tax where the New York base is fully eliminated.

The model used an estimated $32,600 standard deduction and estimated brackets instead of the engine's $33,269.77 taxable-income deduction and exact 2026 rate schedule. It also omitted the $5,721.98 NIIT, leaving its total below the required three-component calculation.

The model invented approximately $110,000 of mortgage interest even though only the mortgage balance was supplied and unlisted expenses are zero. Its $249,914 result also does not follow from its own stated taxable income or preferential-rate treatment and incorrectly describes employer insurance premiums as nonrefundable credits.

The model explicitly excluded NIIT from the requested federal income-tax output, omitting $5,721.98. It then reported $91,458 despite its own regular-tax arithmetic yielding about $102,907, so the submitted value discarded part of its calculated tax as well.

The model fabricated mortgage interest from an assumed 7% rate and used an unsupported $60,000 itemized deduction. Its $154,430 result is also arithmetically disconnected from the roughly $92,000 ordinary tax and $15,584 dividend tax it stated.

The model treated 2026 as a post-TCJA reversion year, applied obsolete 15%–39.6% brackets and AMT rules, and invented mortgage interest using an assumed 7% rate. The applicable calculation instead uses the exact 2026 schedule, $33,269.77 of deductions, and no added AMT component.

The model invented approximately $28,000 of mortgage interest from a balance with no reported interest expense, producing an unsupported $40,574 itemized deduction. It then used approximate brackets and failed to separately compute the $5,721.98 NIIT.

The model incorrectly made a $131,247 tentative AMT control the liability. The correct computation has $89,095.92 of ordinary tax, $11,688 of preferential-rate tax, and $5,721.98 of NIIT, with no such AMT addition.

The model applied expired-law assumptions, including uncapped SALT, a mortgage-interest deduction on an inferred $1 million debt portion, and personal exemptions. These unsupported deductions reduced ordinary tax to $88,016 instead of the trace's $89,095.92.

The stated $460,936 AGI omits substantial taxable investment income: the trace has $541,928.34 of gross income before deductions. The model consequently applied the brackets to the wrong income base and did not establish the separate $11,688 preferential tax and $5,721.98 NIIT.

The model used an AGI of roughly $525,049 rather than deriving $541,928.34 of gross income and $503,494.75 of taxable income. It also added an AMT component that is absent from the correct three-part liability.

The model incorrectly assumed expiration of the applicable individual provisions and used full SALT deductions and rates reaching 33%. The exact 2026 rules yield $89,095.92 of ordinary tax before adding $11,688 of preferential tax and $5,721.98 of NIIT.
The model used the 2025 standard deduction and 2025 brackets for a 2026 household. It also omitted the $5,721.98 NIIT, while the trace uses $33,269.77 of deductions and the exact 2026 rate schedule.

The $21,735 answer captures only a small fraction of the liability and is inconsistent with the model's own acknowledgment that both ordinary income and qualified dividends remain taxable. Applying the brackets to $503,494.75 and adding preferential tax and NIIT yields the three components totaling $106,505.90.

The model invoked unspecified itemized deductions, including real-estate and medical deductions, without deriving them under the applicable floors and limits. Its $75,657 answer omits over $30,000 of the ordinary, preferential-rate, and NIIT liability produced by the trace.

The model used an estimated $32,200 standard deduction and then improperly added a separate $1,070 nonitemizer charitable deduction, producing $505,148 rather than $503,494.75 of taxable income. That wrong deduction base raised regular tax enough to exceed the trace despite computing NIIT nearly exactly.

The model described the right tax components but applied an inexact 2026 deduction or bracket schedule. The exact components are $89,095.92 ordinary tax, $11,688 preferential-rate tax, and $5,721.98 NIIT, not $104,535.

The model said the household itemized without identifying deductions that produce the trace's $33,269.77 taxable-income deduction. Its resulting tax is $632.10 too high because the taxable-income deduction or exact bracket computation was misapplied.

The model identified the standard deduction, qualified-dividend treatment, and NIIT but did not use the exact 2026 deduction and rate parameters. Its calculation exceeds the trace by $871.10 instead of summing the exact $89,095.92, $11,688, and $5,721.98 components.

The model introduced a qualified-business-income deduction even though the household has no listed qualified business income. It also relied on unspecified mortgage interest and credits, causing an unsupported $112,450 residual rather than the trace's three-component tax.

The model applied post-sunset Pease, personal-exemption, miscellaneous-deduction, and AMT rules that do not govern this 2026 calculation. Its erroneous $125,199 tentative AMT replaced the correct $89,095.92 ordinary-bracket tax.

The model subtracted $13,369 of employer health premiums from wages even though gross wages and salaries were specified as annual gross amounts and the trace's employment-income adjustment is the $41,650 allowed 401(k) contribution. It also applied obsolete full property-tax and miscellaneous-itemized deductions, producing the wrong AGI, taxable income, and ordinary tax.
The model supplied no numeric output or explanation for the requested variable. The required parsed value therefore was entirely missing.

The model invented enough mortgage interest and other itemized deductions to erase taxable income, despite the instruction that unlisted expenses are zero. The listed deductions leave $503,494.75 taxable, so neither the $2.85 million mortgage balance nor the capital loss eliminates the tax liability.

The model invented mortgage interest using an assumed 5.75% rate and incorrectly treated the $10,911 net capital loss as reducing preferential-rate income to $67,009 rather than allowing a $3,000 ordinary loss while retaining $77,920 of adjusted net capital gains from qualified dividends. It also omitted the $5,721.98 NIIT.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The model used projected rather than applicable 2026 Social Security parameters and calculated California SDI as $5,160 instead of $5,590. It then submitted $33,296.55 despite its own corrected calculation of $35,558.20, so its final value also violated its stated computation.

The model incorrectly capped California SDI at $153,164 per spouse and added employer ETT and $10,086.52 of state income tax to an employee-payroll-tax output. California SDI contributes $5,590 here, while state income tax and employer payroll taxes are excluded.

The model used an estimated $181,800 Social Security wage base and a 1.2% California SDI rate. The applicable calculations produce $22,599 of Social Security tax and $5,590 of SDI, bringing the total to $36,044.

The model identified $22,599 of Social Security, $6,235 of Medicare, and $1,620 of Additional Medicare Tax but submitted less than even those federal components and omitted California SDI from the final value. The required $5,590 SDI component raises the total to $36,044.

The model used a $176,100 Social Security wage base and a 1.2% California SDI rate. Those obsolete or estimated parameters understated both the $22,599 Social Security component and the $5,590 SDI component.
